Party_Details.Name: Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd (acting by Carolyn Fong in S 477/2012)

Facts.Text: Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd is a Singapore-incorporated company referred to in the judgment as AT.
Suit No 477 of 2012 is a derivative action brought by shareholder Carolyn Fong on behalf of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd against Linda Kao Chai-Chau.

In Originating Summons No 505 of 2010, the High Court granted Carolyn Fong leave under section 216A of the Companies Act to institute a derivative action on behalf of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd against Linda Kao Chai-Chau.

At the date of Peter Fong's death, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd had its shares held by the Fong Foundation charitable trust as to about 51%, Peter Fong as to about 3.6%, Carolyn Fong as to about 6.2%, and 11 other persons holding the balance in varying amounts.

Suit No 477 of 2012 was commenced as a claim by Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, acting through a derivative action by Carolyn Fong, against Linda Kao Chai-Chau in respect of transactions mostly taking place while Peter Fong was alive and Chairman of the company.
In Suit No 477 of 2012, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, acting by Carolyn Fong, alleges that certain business opportunities were diverted away from the company by Linda Kao Chai-Chau.

In Fong Wai Lyn Carolyn v Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd and another [2011] 3 SLR 980, the High Court described Peter Fong as the controlling mind and will of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd at the material times for the leave application.
The Court of Appeal subsequently affirmed the High Court's decision to grant leave to Carolyn Fong to commence the derivative action on behalf of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.

After the appeals on the third party proceedings were heard, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by Carolyn Fong applied for leave to hand over conduct of the Derivative Action to the Receivers and Managers.
In submissions, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by Carolyn Fong accepted that Peter Fong had at least ostensible authority to give instructions on behalf of the company.

In submissions, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by Carolyn Fong accepted that Peter Fong was the alter ego and controlling will of the company up till his death for the purposes of the leave application.
Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by Carolyn Fong opposed Linda Kao Chai-Chau’s application for leave to serve a third party notice against the Estate of Peter Fong in the Derivative Action.

Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by Carolyn Fong objected to Summons No 2990 of 2013, in which Linda applied for leave to serve a third party notice against the Estate of Peter Fong for an indemnity.

Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by Carolyn Fong made submissions before the Assistant Registrar and on appeal that the proposed third party claim against the Estate of Peter Fong was unnecessary and barred by illegality.

In its submissions, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by Carolyn Fong argued that an indemnity could not be claimed in respect of conduct amounting to an offence under section 157 of the Companies Act.
Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by Carolyn Fong relied on the common law authority of Burrows v Rhodes and Jameson [1899] 1 QB 816 to support its position on illegality and indemnity.

Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by Carolyn Fong submitted that the third party proceedings would be redundant because the claim against the Estate of Peter Fong replicated the core of Linda Kao Chai-Chau’s defence in the two suits.
Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by Carolyn Fong took the position that Peter Fong did not know of certain transactions in question and or that those transactions were unauthorised.

Counsel for Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by Carolyn Fong informed the court that they accepted Peter Fong was the alter ego and controlling will of the company up till his death in the context of the earlier leave application.
Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by Carolyn Fong argued that the third party proceedings could embarrass or delay the company and were unnecessary given the overlap with the pleaded defence.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, in the context of Suit No 477 of 2012, the defendant managing director can maintain third party proceedings for indemnity or contribution against the Estate of Peter Fong in respect of alleged diversions of business opportunities and related conduct, and whether such third party proceedings are barred by illegality or are redundant given the pleaded defence.
Party_Details.Rule: Order 16 rule 1 of the Rules of Court governs the issuing of third party notices where a defendant claims contribution or indemnity or related relief against a non-party; sections 15, 16 and 19 of the Civil Law Act establish a statutory right of contribution between persons liable in respect of the same damage, regardless of whether liability arises in tort, contract, breach of trust or otherwise; the common law maxim ex turpi causa non oritur actio limits enforcement of indemnities for manifestly unlawful acts, subject to the statutory contribution regime; and company law principles provide that directors owe fiduciary and statutory duties to the company, that consent or acquiescence of shareholders may in some circumstances authorise or ratify conduct, and that knowledge of a director acting in fraud of the company may not be imputed to the company when it sues its officers for breach of duty.
Party_Details.Application: Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by Carolyn Fong relied on the ex turpi causa principle and Burrows v Rhodes to argue that any indemnity claim by Linda against the Estate of Peter Fong in respect of alleged breaches of fiduciary and statutory duties was barred, and further contended that the proposed third party claim merely duplicated Linda's defence that she acted on Peter Fong's instructions, so that if that defence succeeded there would be no liability to the company and if it failed there would be no basis for contribution; the court considered these submissions in light of the statutory contribution regime under sections 15 and 16 of the Civil Law Act, which allows contribution between persons liable for the same damage even where the underlying conduct may involve torts or crimes, and examined whether there were factual scenarios in which Linda could be held liable to the company while the Estate of Peter Fong could also be liable in respect of the same loss, such that a non-redundant contribution claim could arise.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The court allowed the commencement and amendment of third party proceedings by Linda against the Estate of Peter Fong, holding that illegality did not bar a statutory contribution claim and that the third party notices were not redundant, while leaving the ultimate allocation of contribution and any exercise of discretion under section 16(2) of the Civil Law Act to the trial court.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court analysed the scope of Order 16 rule 1 of the Rules of Court and the statutory contribution provisions in sections 15, 16 and 19 of the Civil Law Act, noting their close similarity to the UK Civil Liability (Contribution) Act 1978 and the legislative intention to align Singapore law with that regime; it reviewed authorities including Burrows v Rhodes, K v P, and Nganthavee to distinguish common law indemnity principles from the statutory right of contribution, and concluded that the ex turpi causa defence does not operate as a bar to a contribution claim under the statute, with any policy concerns instead addressed through the court's discretion to apportion or exempt contribution under section 16(2); the court then considered whether the third party proceedings were redundant by examining Canadian and Singapore cases such as Yemen, Westcoast, Nganthavee, and Panweld, and reasoned that unlike situations where the third party's conduct is fully attributable to the plaintiff or where shareholder consent would negate any breach, there were realistic scenarios here in which Linda could be held to have breached independent duties to the company while Peter Fong, as Chairman and director, might also be liable for directing or authorising the impugned transactions, particularly given uncertainties about his status as alter ego at different times and the dispersed shareholding; on that basis, the court inferred that the third party notices raised a genuine issue of potential concurrent liability for the same damage and should not be struck out at the interlocutory stage, leaving questions of factual findings, any benefit retained by Linda, and the possible exercise of the section 16(2) discretion to be determined at trial.

Party_Details.Name: Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd (acting by Receivers and Managers in S 1015/2012)

Facts.Text: In Suit No 1015 of 2012, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd is represented by its Receivers and Managers appointed from Ernst & Young.
On 17 January 2012, Ernst & Young was appointed as Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd pursuant to an application by shareholder Carolyn Fong.
The appointment of the Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd was made by a consent order following an apparent agreement between Carolyn Fong and Linda Kao Chai-Chau.
After their appointment, the Receivers and Managers reviewed the affairs of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd and decided to commence Suit No 1015 of 2012.

The Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd took the view that there was evidence of diversions of business and funds involving Linda Kao Chai-Chau and others to the detriment of the company, largely concerning transactions or events occurring after the death of Peter Fong.

In Suit No 1015 of 2012, the Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd allege that Linda Kao Chai-Chau and 15 other individuals and corporate entities were involved in a conspiracy to divert business away from the company and in breaches of fiduciary duty.
In the RM Action, Linda Kao Chai-Chau commenced third party proceedings against the Estate of Peter Fong on 19 April 2013 seeking an indemnity.
On 12 June 2013, the Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd filed Summons No 2989 of 2013 to set aside the third party proceedings brought by Linda against the Estate of Peter Fong.
On 19 July 2013, the Assistant Registrar granted Summons No 2989 of 2013 and set aside the third party notice in the RM Action.

The Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd opposed the continuation of third party proceedings against the Estate of Peter Fong in the RM Action on the basis of illegality and redundancy.

In their submissions, the Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd argued that alleged breaches of directors' duties in the RM Action could amount to offences under section 157 of the Companies Act and possibly under provisions of the Penal Code relating to dishonest misappropriation of property and criminal breach of trust.

In the RM Action, the Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd expressly denied that Peter Fong was the alter ego or controlling mind of the company at the material times for the alleged post-death transactions.

The Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd submitted that the third party proceedings were unnecessary because Linda's defence that she acted on Peter Fong's instructions, if accepted, would negate liability to the company, and if rejected, would leave no basis for contribution.

In their closing submissions, the Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd argued that the factual premise of Linda's third party claim, namely that Peter Fong would have instructed conduct causing the company to lose almost its entire business, was inherently improbable.

The Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd relied on Order 18 rule 19 of the Rules of Court to support their application to strike out the third party claim as disclosing no reasonable cause of action or as being an abuse of process.

The Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd took the position that Linda, as managing director, owed an independent duty to the company and could not avoid that duty merely by following instructions of the Chairman and majority shareholder.

In the later application by Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by Carolyn Fong to hand over conduct of the Derivative Action to the Receivers and Managers, the Receivers and Managers adopted a neutral position.

Counsel for the Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd made submissions on 2 September 2013 and 11 October 2013 addressing the legal question posed by the court regarding the effect of a controlling mind's instructions on another director's liability.

The Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd submitted that any contribution claim by Linda against the Estate of Peter Fong should, if allowed at all, be addressed by the trial court through the discretion under section 16(2) of the Civil Law Act after full ventilation of the facts.
The Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd argued that the third party proceedings would cause additional delay and expense to the main action.

Party_Details.Issue: In Suit No 1015 of 2012, whether the defendant managing director may maintain third party proceedings for indemnity or contribution against the Estate of Peter Fong in respect of alleged conspiratorial diversions of business and breaches of duty occurring largely after Peter Fong's death, and whether such proceedings should be struck out as barred by illegality, disclosing no reasonable cause of action, or being redundant or an abuse of process.
Party_Details.Rule: Order 16 rule 1 of the Rules of Court permits a defendant to issue a third party notice where contribution or indemnity or related relief is claimed against a non-party; Order 18 rule 19 allows the court to strike out pleadings that disclose no reasonable cause of action, are scandalous, frivolous or vexatious, may prejudice, embarrass or delay the fair trial, or are an abuse of process; sections 15, 16 and 19 of the Civil Law Act create a statutory right of contribution between persons liable in respect of the same damage, irrespective of whether liability arises in tort, contract, breach of trust or otherwise, and confer a discretion on the court to apportion or exempt contribution; and company law and agency principles govern directors' independent duties to the company and the circumstances in which instructions from a controlling shareholder or chairman may or may not affect those duties.
Party_Details.Application: The Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd invoked Order 18 rule 19 to argue that Linda's third party claim against the Estate of Peter Fong in the RM Action was unnecessary and legally untenable, contending that any alleged instructions from Peter Fong could not justify an indemnity for conduct potentially amounting to statutory and criminal offences and that, in any event, Linda's defence and third party claim rested on the same factual premise, making the third party proceedings redundant; the court assessed these contentions against the statutory framework of sections 15 and 16 of the Civil Law Act, which allow contribution between persons liable for the same damage even where the underlying conduct may involve illegality, and considered whether there were plausible factual scenarios in which Linda could be held liable for breaches of her independent duties as managing director in relation to post-death transactions while the Estate of Peter Fong might also bear liability for the same loss, concluding that such scenarios could not be excluded at the interlocutory stage and that any ultimate adjustment, including possible exemption of contribution, should be left to the trial court's discretion.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The court declined to strike out the third party proceedings in the RM Action and allowed Linda to pursue contribution claims against the Estate of Peter Fong, including amendments to add contribution, while reserving questions of liability apportionment and any exercise of the section 16(2) Civil Law Act discretion to the trial judge.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: In relation to the RM Action, the court considered the Receivers and Managers' reliance on Order 18 rule 19 and their submissions on illegality and redundancy, but interpreted sections 15 and 16 of the Civil Law Act as providing a broad statutory right of contribution that is not negated by the mere presence of alleged criminal or statutory breaches, drawing support from analogous UK and Singapore authorities; the court evaluated whether the third party claim simply duplicated Linda's defence and, by examining the temporal context of the alleged diversions, Peter Fong's reduced shareholding at the time of his death, and the Receivers and Managers' denial that he was the alter ego for the relevant period, reasoned that it was possible for Linda to be held liable for failing to discharge her independent duties as managing director while the Estate of Peter Fong might also be liable in respect of the same damage, thereby justifying a contribution claim; the court further reasoned that concerns about unjust enrichment or policy objections linked to ex turpi causa could be addressed by the trial court through the discretionary power under section 16(2) to adjust or deny contribution after full factual findings, and concluded that striking out the third party proceedings at this stage would be premature given the close connection between the main claims and the contribution issues.
